sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.content: Risk factors for vulvar neoplasia include increasing age (median diagnosis age 65-70), human papillomavirus (HPV) infection (attributable risk 39.7% based on meta-analysis), cigarette smoking, inflammatory conditions of the vulva (e.g., lichen sclerosus), immunodeficiency (including HIV), and HPV-associated lower genital tract neoplasia. Attributable risk is specifically quantified for HPV infection at 39.7%.

cards.detail: Most vulvar neoplasia are diagnosed at early stages in older populations. The median age of diagnosis for invasive vulvar cancer is 65-70 years [MS-2]. Age is an independent prognostic factor and treatment modality varies with age.

Estimates of the percentage of vulvar cancers attributable to HPV infection range from 30% to 69%, with a meta-analysis reporting an HPV prevalence of 39.7% [MS-2]. HPV-16 is the predominant type (78.1% of HPV-positive disease), followed by HPV-33 (7.5%) [MS-2]. HPV-associated SCC occurs more frequently in younger patients and is multifocal.
Cigarette smoking is listed as a recognized risk factor for the development of vulvar neoplasia [MS-1]. Smoking cessation counseling is recommended in the workup pathway [VULVA-1].

cards.strength: Established; lichen sclerosus is specifically identified as associated with HPV-independent SCC [VULVA-A 1/4, VULVA-C 1/6]
cards.detail: HPV-independent VIN and SCC are identified in the setting of chronic vulvar inflammatory disorders such as lichen sclerosus [VULVA-A 1/4]. The presence of dVIN and lichen sclerosus may also play a significant role in recurrence or development of new primary carcinomas even with negative surgical margins [VULVA-C 1/6].

Immunodeficiency, including HIV infection, is listed as a risk factor for the development of vulvar neoplasia [MS-1]. The NCCN guidelines recommend considering HIV testing, especially in younger patients suspected of having SCC of the vulva or other HPV-related disease [VULVA-1]. Patients with vulvar cancer and HIV should be referred to an HIV specialist, and modifications to cancer treatment should not be made solely on the basis of HIV status [VULVA-1].

HPV-associated SCC can be seen in conjunction with additional sites of lower genital tract squamous neoplasia, including cervical neoplasia. Concurrent cervical HPV and cytology testing is recommended in the workup [VULVA-1].
